Tafseer:
The meaning of 'Infitar' is to split and tear open. The splitting of the sky at the occurrence of the Resurrection has been mentioned in various places in the Qur'an. In the very first verse of Surah Al-Inshiqaq, this same subject is described by: (When the sky splits open). In Surah Ar-Rahman, verse 37, the word 'Inshiqaq' is also used, and 'Infitar' and 'Inshiqaq' are synonymous.
A new world with new laws: After the Resurrection, a completely new world will appear under new laws and systems; therefore, everything in this old world will break and disintegrate. What will be the nature of this disintegration? A correct concept of it cannot be formed today, but its reminder is given to shake the wealthy and arrogant who sit completely untroubled based on their trust in their fortresses and strongholds, assuming that what they have built is sufficient to keep them safe from every danger. They are reminded: the upheaval of the Resurrection will be such that, let alone your small shelters, this entire firm roof of the sky—in which you cannot find a single flaw today—will become completely filled with rifts and cracks.
Do not distress your mind here with the question of whether this sky we see is merely a void or a solid entity; rather, believe that just as you witness it today as a firm ceiling in which no crack can be pointed out, similarly at the time of the Resurrection's upheaval, it will appear filled with cracks.

Disintegration of the stellar system:
'Intithar' means to scatter, disperse, and be thrown about. That is: while today the stars appear fixed like lamps in a firm, unseen structure of the sky's ceiling, on that Day, this structure will disintegrate and they will fall and be scattered in all directions. In the previous Surah, their losing of light was mentioned because, due to the sun being wrapped up, their connection with the solar system will end. In this Surah, their scattering is mentioned because the canopy itself—for the decoration of which they were hung—will no longer exist.

The state of the earth's seas and its graves:
After describing the state of the heaven and its stars, this mentions the state of two things of the earth—the seas and the graves—as examples, showing that on that Day, the seas will burst forth from their boundaries and those buried in the graves will be brought out of them.
In the previous Surah, the words: (And when the seas are erupted) (81:6) appeared, and here, the same is described by 'fujjirat' (burst forth). The only difference is that the first word highlights the boiling and raging of the seas, while the second highlights their freedom and release from boundaries. That is, they will break free from their present boundaries and flow everywhere, covering all high and low lands. This word was explained under Surah Ad-Dahr, verse 6: (They cause it to gush forth abundantly).

'And when the graves are scattered' (wa idha-l-qubooru bu'thirat) - 'Ba'thara-sh-shay'' means: he scattered and dispersed something, laid it open, or opened it to extract whatever was inside. Although the graves are mentioned specifically here because, from the perspective of warning, primary importance belonged to opening them and bringing out the people from them, other places of the Qur'an clarify that on that Day, the earth will cast out its entire burden, as stated in Surah Az-Zalzalah:
(And the earth discharges its burdens) (99:2),
and in Surah Al-Inshiqaq:
(And when the earth is extended, and has cast out what was within it and become empty) (84:3-4).

This states the core reality that will face everyone on that Day. That is: when this great upheaval occurs in the universe, of which some signs have been mentioned, then every person will know what they sent before and what they left behind. The meaning is: those who are mocking the Prophet's warning today should not remain in the delusion that these days will continue forever, but should reflect on their end keeping in view the great upheaval of that Day—which everyone must face, and from which no one, great or small, will find any refuge.
The interpretation of 'qaddama' and 'akhkhara': If 'ma qaddama wa akhkhara' (what he sent before and left behind) is interpreted keeping in view the arrogant leaders who are the primary addressees of the Surah, the meaning is: they will see the consequence of the evil deeds they committed against Allah and His Messenger, and they will suffer the regret of the righteous deeds they ignored. In Surah Al-Jumu'ah, He states regarding the Jews:
(And they will never wish for it, ever, because of what their hands have sent before [qaddamat]) (62:7).
That is: they know well the record they have sent before for the Hereafter; therefore, they do not possess the courage to present themselves before God. Similarly, the Qur'an repeatedly states that on the Day of Resurrection, the disbelievers will say with deep regret: 'O, would that we had sent before some good deeds for the life of the Hereafter!' In Surah Al-Fajr, their statement is recorded as:
(He will say, 'Oh, I wish I had sent ahead [qaddamtu] for my life!') (89:24),
and in Surah Al-Mu'minun:
(Until, when death comes to one of them, he says, 'My Lord, send me back that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind [taraktu]') (23:99-100).
Although these references clarify the true meaning of both 'qaddama' and 'akhkhara', some have interpreted it as:
(meaning: the good and evil he performed, and what he left undone).
Similarly, others have interpreted it as: (what he spent from his wealth for his Hereafter, and what he left behind for his heirs). Although the general wording can accommodate these meanings, the first interpretation is preferred in our view because of the context of the discourse.

A lethal delusion from God's generosity:
Although 'insan' (human) is general, the addressees are those deniers of the Resurrection who are warned in this Surah. Thus, in the subsequent verse, He addresses them directly: (No! But you deny the Recompense) (verse 9). We repeatedly explain the eloquence of addressing specific targets through a general word.
'O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous?' (ya ayyuha-l-insanu ma gharraka bi-rabbika-l-kareem) - The interrogative style here is to express amazement. The meaning is: if your Lord's generosity has made you secure from His recompense—since He does not seize you immediately for your rebelliousness but grants you respite upon respite—you have suffered a highly severe delusion from His generosity. You should have appreciated His grace, become His grateful servants, and made yourselves worthy of His further favors; but instead, you became completely bold before Him, mocking His warning and assuming that the prosperity you enjoy is your birthright, and the Resurrection of which the Messenger is warning is merely a fantasy.

Referencing those attributes of Allah that prove the recompense:
Here, the Generous Lord has referenced His attributes manifest in the creation of man, which serve as proof that the Lord Who displayed His care, craftsmanship, wisdom, and power in human creation did not create him in vain to wander like an unbridled beast. Rather, He will inevitably bring him before Him one day, take his account, bless His obedient servants with eternal mercy, and cast the rebellious into Hell. If this were not to happen, all of this care would become meaningless, and His power and wisdom manifest in His every act would be negated.
'Who created you, then proportioned you' (alladhi khalaqaka fa-sawwak) - 'Khalaq' means creating and preparing the basic form, and 'taswiyah' means proportioning and shaping. This points to both the initial and final stages of human creation: the same Generous Lord created your basic form, and He proportioned your shape. 'And balanced you' (fa-'adalak) - and He made you a balanced creation.
The presence of wisdom in human creation is a proof that he is not a plaything: As noted, this is intended to draw attention to the care and favor manifest in human creation, which is a proof that man is not a plaything whom His power created for temporary amusement, to be broken and discarded as He pleases. The more care is spent on something, the greater is its purpose, and the greater is its status in the system of creation. Man is not like insects that appear in the rain and perish; rather, he is the pinnacle of His craftsmanship; therefore, he must pass through trials to reach the status destined for him, and if he fails to show courage, he must face the punishment for his failure.
'Fa-'adalak' points to the balance and proportion described in: (Indeed, We created man in the best of molds) (At-Tin, 95:4). Man, in his physical form and his spiritual and intellectual capabilities, stands at the exact center of all creation; therefore, he became worthy of being made God's representative on the earth, being honored with the status of a balanced nation, and if he fulfills the right of God's representation, being made worthy of the eternal kingdom of heaven.

'In whatever form He willed, He assembled you' (fi ayyi sooratim-ma sha'a rakkabak) - That is: on one hand, reflect on your Lord's care and favor in prescribing a distinct shape for every individual, assembling him in the form He willed by His perfect power. He faced no difficulty in this; indeed, one cannot find even two individuals who share the exact same physical form among millions of humans.
The meaning is: for the God Whose power and favor are of this level, it is necessary that He distinguishes between your righteous and wicked one day, and He will resurrect you after death for this task, which is in no way difficult for Him.

Rebuking the deniers of the Resurrection:
'No!' (Kalla) refutes those doubts and objections which the deniers raised against the Resurrection, and which were refuted in the preceding verses through His attributes manifest in human creation. Having presented them, these builders of doubts are rebuked: 'Kalla'—meaning: your doubts and objections have no foundation, and are completely fabricated. The actual reality is that you do not want to believe in recompense; therefore, you are raising absurd doubts as to how people can be resurrected after they have decayed. Yet, if recompense is necessary according to intellect, justice, nature, and His mercy and wisdom, why should resurrecting them from the graves be difficult!
Note that occasionally a person wants to deny something but finds no ground to oppose it directly; therefore, he raises irrelevant questions to create doubts. The deniers of the Quraysh were trapped in this very confusion. They knew that denying the Resurrection is equivalent to denying an obvious truth, but they were not ready to accept it; therefore, they hid behind fabricated doubts to pretend they had arguments for not accepting the Qur'an's warning.

This relates to the rebuke in the preceding verse, and warns those who raised artificial doubts regarding the account of the Hereafter.
Those appointed to record the deeds: It is stated: do not remain in the delusion that no one can be aware of your secret and public deeds to account for them. Allah Almighty has appointed guardians over you who are recording your every word, and who hear and know whatever you say or do. Furthermore, they are highly noble.

Describing these angels as 'noble' (kiram) is to remind of the reality that they are performing their assigned duty with complete dedication, sense of responsibility, and absolute impartiality. They neither show any negligence, nor behave with compromise, nor are they influenced by anyone's pressure or flattery to show partiality.

'They know whatever you do' (ya'lamoona ma taf'aloon) - That is: whatever you do and wherever you do it is clear to them. While only the knowing of deeds is mentioned here, Surah Qaf states:
(Not a word does he utter but there is with him a watcher ready) (Qaf, 50:18).
Surah Qaf also clarifies that these angels are two, guarding from the right and left. Hadiths also clarify that they share the work: one is appointed to record good deeds, and the other to record evil deeds.

The consequence of this arrangement and system:
This states the consequence of the arrangement and system described above—showing that it is a proof that Allah Almighty will not treat the righteous and the wicked in the same manner; rather, He will admit the righteous into Paradise and cast the wicked into Hell. Those foolish individuals who assume life is only the life of this world, or dream that if there is a Hereafter, they will receive a better life through the intercession of their partners, should correct their thinking. The Creator of this universe is not indifferent or impartial regarding good and evil; He will inevitably distinguish between them and recompense each according to his deeds. If this were not to happen, it would mean that the righteous and the wicked are equal in His sight, and His world is an unjust place where there is no concept of truth and justice.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 13 and 14 was combined in the original text.]

Facing the actual reality:
That is: instead of living in such sweet dreams, people should face the actual reality. On the Day of Recompense, all the wicked will enter Hell, and they will not be able to be absent from it even for a moment.

'And they are not from it to be absent' (wa ma hum 'anha bi-gha'ibeen) has the same meaning as 'abiding therein eternally'. The meaning is: if anyone assumes that if they dislike the place, they can find a way to escape, they should discard this thought. Once they enter, all ways of escape will be closed.

This question is to express the grandeur and importance of the Day of Recompense, and its repetition makes it even more terrifying. Address in 'yudreeka' (what can make you know) is not to the Prophet, but to those who are addressed in: 'O man, what has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous?' and the subsequent verses. We have explained the eloquence of addressing a plural target through a singular pronoun.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 17 and 18 was combined in the original text.]

Since the question was intended solely to portray the terror of that Day, He answered it Himself without waiting for a response: on that Day, no soul will have power to do anything for another soul. The entire authority and decision on that Day will belong only to Allah, the One without partners. No one will be able to bring any benefit to another. Those who were regarded as partners and intercessors in the hope that they would save them from God's grasp will completely disappear.
